reviews.summary: The embedding space used for calculating FID is computed with Inception V3 which is trained for image classification as the target task. This means it is more likely to capture discriminative features, raising doubts about its ability to effectively evaluate generative models. There is also a need to devise a new evaluation metric that can interpret underlying factors. This paper introduces a method called Directional CLIP Score (DCS) to properly evaluate this. The pre-trained CLIP is used as the embedding space. In particular, the paper proposes "Single attribute KL div (SaKLD)" to measure single attribute alignment and "Paired attribute KL div (PaKLD)" to measure multiple attribute alignment as new metrics. This paper provides some insightful measurement result using the proposed metrics.
reviews.strengths: The effort to evaluate generative models from various perspectives through the introduction of such metrics can be considered novel and a contribution. Especially considering the current issues such as the bias in stable diffusion [1], the proposal of such metrics can bring benefits to the field from the perspective of trustworthy AI.
reviews.weaknesses:
It appears to be an intrinsic limitation that a significant number of samples (50k) are still required to obtain stable results. Nonetheless, thanks to the reported findings, we can gain more insights, and I appreciate that. 
Remaining concerns are written in [Questions] section.
reviews.questions:
1) In lines 96-97, it seems that the authors of the referenced paper already propose using the CLIP embedding space for evaluating generative model? If not, it would be helpful to clarify the differences compared to previously proposed methods. Regarding the for explainable evaluation in the related work section, it is necessary to determine whether this paper is the first to come up with it. If there are any additional previous works, it would be helpful to share the, 

2) The major concern raised is the reliance on the embedding space. What if there are biases in the CLIP space itself? For example, if being female and wearing makeup are highly correlated, even a woman who does not actually wear makeup may show high similarity to makeup attribute. In such cases, it becomes difficult to consider CLIP score or CLIP direction score as accurate measures of similarity. While a more disentangled multimodal latent space may help alleviate this problem, I'm curious about the authors' perspective on this issue.

3) Directional CLIP Score adopts a method of computing the center of training images and normalizing them. Does this mean that anyone who wants to evaluate a generative model needs to share all these training images? Also, considering the introduction of the auxiliary image captioning model called BLIP, the improvement in performance seems minimal. Do the authors believe it is worth the trade-off?

4) Question about Table 5: cI may have missed it, but was there an explanation for the last column of FID_clip in Table 5? What does it represent? Also, in Table 5, it seems that SaKLD is calculated for a single attribute. How is it computed?

5) Can PaKLD be proposed for more than two attributes? It would be great if judgments could be made for a wide range of attributes as shown in Figure 1 (b).

6) How should we interpret numerical values itself of the scores?  Although it may not be as interpretable as accuracy I'm still curious about the authors' insights based on their experience.
reviews.limitations: It seems that there are no particular specific limitations.
reviews.rating: 6: Weak Accept: Technically solid, moderate-to-high impact paper, with no major concerns with respect to evaluation, resources, reproducibility, ethical considerations.
reviews.confidence: 4: You are confident in your assessment, but not absolutely certain. It is unlikely, but not impossible, that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work.
reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
The embedding space used for calculating FID is computed with Inception V3 which is trained for image classification as the target task. This means it is more likely to capture discriminative features, raising doubts about its ability to effectively evaluate generative models. There is also a need to devise a new evaluation metric that can interpret underlying factors. This paper introduces a method called Directional CLIP Score (DCS) to properly evaluate this. The pre-trained CLIP is used as the embedding space. In particular, the paper proposes "Single attribute KL div (SaKLD)" to measure single attribute alignment and "Paired attribute KL div (PaKLD)" to measure multiple attribute alignment as new metrics. This paper provides some insightful measurement result using the proposed metrics.

Strengths:
The effort to evaluate generative models from various perspectives through the introduction of such metrics can be considered novel and a contribution. Especially considering the current issues such as the bias in stable diffusion [1], the proposal of such metrics can bring benefits to the field from the perspective of trustworthy AI.

Weaknesses:
It appears to be an intrinsic limitation that a significant number of samples (50k) are still required to obtain stable results. Nonetheless, thanks to the reported findings, we can gain more insights, and I appreciate that. 
Remaining concerns are written in [Questions] section.

Questions:
1) In lines 96-97, it seems that the authors of the referenced paper already propose using the CLIP embedding space for evaluating generative model? If not, it would be helpful to clarify the differences compared to previously proposed methods. Regarding the for explainable evaluation in the related work section, it is necessary to determine whether this paper is the first to come up with it. If there are any additional previous works, it would be helpful to share the, 

2) The major concern raised is the reliance on the embedding space. What if there are biases in the CLIP space itself? For example, if being female and wearing makeup are highly correlated, even a woman who does not actually wear makeup may show high similarity to makeup attribute. In such cases, it becomes difficult to consider CLIP score or CLIP direction score as accurate measures of similarity. While a more disentangled multimodal latent space may help alleviate this problem, I'm curious about the authors' perspective on this issue.

3) Directional CLIP Score adopts a method of computing the center of training images and normalizing them. Does this mean that anyone who wants to evaluate a generative model needs to share all these training images? Also, considering the introduction of the auxiliary image captioning model called BLIP, the improvement in performance seems minimal. Do the authors believe it is worth the trade-off?

4) Question about Table 5: cI may have missed it, but was there an explanation for the last column of FID_clip in Table 5? What does it represent? Also, in Table 5, it seems that SaKLD is calculated for a single attribute. How is it computed?

5) Can PaKLD be proposed for more than two attributes? It would be great if judgments could be made for a wide range of attributes as shown in Figure 1 (b).

6) How should we interpret numerical values itself of the scores?  Although it may not be as interpretable as accuracy I'm still curious about the authors' insights based on their experience.

Limitations:
It seems that there are no particular specific limitations.

Rating:
6: Weak Accept: Technically solid, moderate-to-high impact paper, with no major concerns with respect to evaluation, resources, reproducibility, ethical considerations.
reviews.text:
## Hybrid Peer Review

**Summary:**
This paper introduces a novel approach to address the critical lack of interpretability in generative model evaluation metrics. Current metrics, like FID, provide aggregate scores but offer limited insight into *why* one model performs better than another. The authors propose "Directional CLIP Score" (DCS), an interpretable embedding method that quantifies image attributes relative to a dataset's center in CLIP space. Leveraging DCS, they introduce two new metrics: Single attribute KL Divergence (SaKLD), which compares individual attribute distributions between generated and real data, and Paired attribute KL Divergence (PaKLD), which assesses the preservation of attribute relationships. The paper provides insightful measurement results using the proposed metrics, demonstrating their superior sensitivity and interpretability compared to FID, and revealing insights into the performance of GANs and Diffusion Models, particularly highlighting Diffusion Models' weaknesses in color attribute generation.

**Strengths:**
The effort to evaluate generative models from various perspectives through the introduction of these metrics is highly original and a significant contribution to the field. The work clearly identifies a crucial gap in existing black-box evaluation approaches and offers a fresh perspective by aligning evaluation with human-like attribute assessment. The concept of Directional CLIPScore (DCS) to create an attribute-driven embedding space is novel, as are the SaKLD and PaKLD metrics, providing deeper, actionable insights into model strengths and weaknesses. Especially considering current issues such as bias in stable diffusion, the proposal of such interpretable metrics can bring substantial benefits from the perspective of trustworthy AI and directly guide model development. The submission is technically sound in its motivation for DCS and the logical progression to KLD-based metrics. The experimental design, including correlated image injection and the PaKLD necessity experiment, effectively validates the proposed metrics' sensitivity and interpretability. The analysis of GANs versus Diffusion Models, particularly the insights into Diffusion Models' performance on color attributes, is well-supported and contributes valuable understanding, making the proposed methods likely to be built upon by researchers aiming for more transparent model evaluation.

**Weaknesses:**
While the proposed approach is promising, several significant concerns regarding its current presentation, technical details, and practical implications need to be addressed.

Firstly, a notable intrinsic limitation is the requirement for a significant number of samples (50k) to obtain stable results, which can be computationally intensive. This concern is compounded by the computational overhead of calculating DCS for numerous attributes, followed by kernel density estimation (KDE) and KL divergence for all single and paired attributes. The paper does not adequately address the computational cost and scalability for large datasets and a high number of attributes compared to simpler metrics like FID, which is a practical concern for widespread adoption. The introduction of the auxiliary image captioning model called BLIP also raises questions about the cost-benefit trade-off, especially if the improvement in performance is minimal.

Secondly, a major concern is the inherent reliance on the embedding space of pre-trained models like CLIP and BLIP. These metrics are tied to the representational biases of these foundational models. If, for example, CLIP has inherent biases (e.g., highly correlating "female" with "wearing makeup"), these biases will propagate directly into the DCS scores, making it difficult to consider them accurate measures of similarity. The quality and representativeness of these attributes directly impact the utility and fairness of the proposed metrics. The paper should explicitly discuss the authors' perspective on these potential biases.

Thirdly, there are several issues impacting clarity, reproducibility, and the perceived technical soundness:
*   **Definition of "Mean Accuracy"**: The "mean accuracy" presented in Table 1 for comparing CLIPScore and DCS is not precisely defined, making it impossible to assess the validity of the claimed superiority of DCS. A detailed explanation of its calculation is needed.
*   **Attribute Selection Process**: For the comparative analysis, the paper mentions using N=20 attributes. The specific process for selecting these attributes, how "frequently appearing" is quantified for BLIP/GPT, and how consistency is maintained across different datasets and attribute selection methods, needs clearer elaboration.
*   **Kernel Density Estimation Details**: Details on the Gaussian kernel density estimation parameters (e.g., bandwidth selection method, how 10,000 points are sampled from the PDF for KLD calculation) are minimal or absent, which impacts the reproducibility and reliability of the KLD calculations.
*   **Definition of CT**: The definition of CT in Equation (2) as the center of BLIP-generated captions for images, rather than directly from the defined attribute texts, lacks clear justification and may introduce unintended dependencies.
*   **Experimental Setup Ambiguity**: The experimental setup in Section 5.1, describing "Correlated Image Injection," is confusing. It states that two non-overlapping subsets of *FFHQ* are considered as 'training data X and generated images Y', implying both are real data, not generated. This contradicts the purpose of evaluating generative models and misses a crucial opportunity to directly demonstrate the metrics' primary intended use.
*   **Contradiction in Experiment**: The 'Necessity of PaKLD' experiment (Section 5.2) contains a direct contradiction between the text (line 252: 'non-smiling men' and 'non-smiling women') and Figure 4's caption ('non-smiling men and smiling women'). Such inconsistencies severely undermine the credibility of the experimental results.
*   **Missing Appendix**: The Appendix is referenced multiple times (lines 189, 276, 294) but is not included, which impedes a full understanding of key methodological choices.
*   **FID_clip**: There is no explanation for the "FID_clip" column in Table 5.

Finally, the generalizability of the metrics beyond human face datasets (FFHQ, CelebA, MetFaces) is not thoroughly explored. Applying these metrics to diverse image domains where attributes are less clearly defined or extractable via text (e.g., abstract patterns, complex scenes, non-human objects) could prove challenging.

**Questions:**

1.  **Prior Work Clarification**: In lines 96-97, it appears the authors of the referenced paper already propose using the CLIP embedding space for evaluating generative models. Please clarify the differences compared to previously proposed methods. Regarding explainable evaluation in the related work section, is this paper the first to come up with it? If there are any additional previous works, it would be helpful to share them.
2.  **CLIP/BLIP Bias**: Given the reliance on CLIP and BLIP embeddings, how do the authors address potential biases within these models that could propagate to the DCS scores, especially when attributes are highly correlated (e.g., "female" and "wearing makeup")? What is the authors' perspective on this issue and how it might affect the accuracy of similarity measures?
3.  **Computational Cost & Trade-offs**: Please provide a detailed analysis of the computational costs (time and memory) for calculating DCS, SaKLD, and PaKLD, especially for large datasets (e.g., 50,000 images) and numerous attributes, including the BLIP captioning, DCS calculation, and kernel density estimation steps. How does this compare to existing metrics like FID? Does the improvement in performance justify the computational trade-off, particularly with the introduction of BLIP?
4.  **DCS Normalization and Data Sharing**: Directional CLIP Score adopts a method of computing the center of training images and normalizing them. Does this imply that anyone who wants to evaluate a generative model needs to share all these training images, raising potential data privacy or access concerns?
5.  **Clarity on "Mean Accuracy"**: Please provide a precise definition of "mean accuracy" as used in Table 1, along with the detailed methodology for its calculation. What constitutes a "positive sample with the highest score" for a given attribute, and how is this aligned with ground truth?
6.  **Attribute Selection Details**: For the comparative analysis and ablation studies, the paper mentions using N=20 attributes. Please clarify which attribute selection method (BLIP, User, or GPT) was primarily used for these experiments, and how the specific set of 20 attributes was chosen and refined.
7.  **Kernel Density Estimation**: Please provide full details on the Gaussian kernel density estimation process used for PDFs, including the bandwidth selection method employed and how the 10,000 points are sampled from each PDF for KLD calculation. How robust are the results to changes in these parameters?
8.  **Definition of CT**: Please clarify the justification for using `CT = 1/N sum(ET(BLIP(xi)))` in Equation (2). Why is the mean of BLIP-generated captions for images the appropriate reference point for attribute texts, rather than a center derived directly from the attribute texts themselves?
9.  **Experimental Setup and Contradictions**:
    *   Please resolve the ambiguity in Section 5.1: Is 'Y' a set of generated images from a specific model, or another real subset of FFHQ? If it's real, how does this experiment directly validate metrics for *generative model* evaluation?
    *   Please correct the contradiction between the text in line 252 ('non-smiling men' and 'non-smiling women') and the caption of Figure 4 ('non-smiling men and smiling women') for the 'Necessity of PaKLD' experiment.
10. **Missing Appendix**: The Appendix is referenced multiple times (lines 189, 276, 294) but is not included. Please provide the Appendix, as it is essential for understanding the complete methodology.
11. **Table 5 Clarification**: What does the last column of "FID\_clip" in Table 5 represent, and how is SaKLD for a single attribute computed in this table?
12. **PaKLD Extensibility**: Can PaKLD be proposed for more than two attributes (e.g., three or more)? It would be valuable if judgments could be made for a wide range of attribute combinations, as implied by Figure 1 (b).
13. **Score Interpretation**: How should we interpret the numerical values of the SaKLD and PaKLD scores themselves? Although they may not be as interpretable as accuracy, what are the authors' insights based on their experience?
14. **Generalizability to Other Domains**: How would the proposed metrics generalize to other domains (e.g., natural scenes, medical images, abstract art) where attributes might be less explicit, require different pre-trained models, or where text-image alignment is less straightforward than with human faces?
15. **Theoretical Justification for DCS**: While empirical results show DCS outperforms CLIPScore, could the authors provide a more theoretical or intuitive explanation for why centering the vectors (Vx, Va) around CX and CT leads to more precise and interpretable attribute scores, beyond the observation of a "narrow distribution" for CLIPScore?

**Limitations:**
While the authors acknowledge that their metrics might yield unreliable results when attributes in images are ambiguous (e.g., complex modern artworks) or when the generative model's output quality is significantly poor, several other critical limitations warrant discussion:
1.  **Dependency on CLIP/BLIP Biases**: The metrics are inherently tied to the representational biases of CLIP and BLIP models. Any biases in these foundational models will propagate directly into the proposed evaluation metrics, potentially affecting their fairness and accuracy.
2.  **Computational Cost and Scalability**: The computational overhead of calculating DCS for numerous images and attributes, followed by kernel density estimation and KL divergence for all single and paired attributes, can be substantial, especially for large datasets and a high number of attributes. This aspect needs thorough discussion.
3.  **Generalizability Beyond Human Faces**: The current validation is largely confined to human face datasets. The generalizability of the metrics to diverse image domains where attributes are less clearly defined or extractable via text remains underexplored.
4.  **Sensitivity to Hyperparameters**: The robustness of the metrics to variations in attribute selection methodology (e.g., specific phrasing of text prompts) and the sensitivity of the kernel density estimation parameters (e.g., kernel bandwidth) are critical but underexplored.

**Rating:**
6: Weak Accept: The paper presents a novel and highly significant approach to interpretable generative model evaluation, addressing a critical gap in the field. The proposed Directional CLIP Score and its derived metrics (SaKLD, PaKLD) offer valuable insights beyond existing black-box metrics. However, the current submission has several significant issues regarding clarity, reproducibility, and the detailed justification of certain methodological choices and experimental setups. These concerns, particularly around the lack of definition for key metrics, ambiguities in experimental descriptions, missing appendices, and unaddressed computational costs, prevent a stronger endorsement. If these major concerns are thoroughly addressed, the paper has the potential for moderate-to-high impact.
reviews.original_text:
Summary:
The embedding space used for calculating FID is computed with Inception V3 which is trained for image classification as the target task. This means it is more likely to capture discriminative features, raising doubts about its ability to effectively evaluate generative models. There is also a need to devise a new evaluation metric that can interpret underlying factors. This paper introduces a method called Directional CLIP Score (DCS) to properly evaluate this. The pre-trained CLIP is used as the embedding space. In particular, the paper proposes "Single attribute KL div (SaKLD)" to measure single attribute alignment and "Paired attribute KL div (PaKLD)" to measure multiple attribute alignment as new metrics. This paper provides some insightful measurement result using the proposed metrics.

Strengths:
The effort to evaluate generative models from various perspectives through the introduction of such metrics can be considered novel and a contribution. Especially considering the current issues such as the bias in stable diffusion [1], the proposal of such metrics can bring benefits to the field from the perspective of trustworthy AI.

Weaknesses:
It appears to be an intrinsic limitation that a significant number of samples (50k) are still required to obtain stable results. Nonetheless, thanks to the reported findings, we can gain more insights, and I appreciate that. 
Remaining concerns are written in [Questions] section.

Questions:
1) In lines 96-97, it seems that the authors of the referenced paper already propose using the CLIP embedding space for evaluating generative model? If not, it would be helpful to clarify the differences compared to previously proposed methods. Regarding the for explainable evaluation in the related work section, it is necessary to determine whether this paper is the first to come up with it. If there are any additional previous works, it would be helpful to share the, 

2) The major concern raised is the reliance on the embedding space. What if there are biases in the CLIP space itself? For example, if being female and wearing makeup are highly correlated, even a woman who does not actually wear makeup may show high similarity to makeup attribute. In such cases, it becomes difficult to consider CLIP score or CLIP direction score as accurate measures of similarity. While a more disentangled multimodal latent space may help alleviate this problem, I'm curious about the authors' perspective on this issue.

3) Directional CLIP Score adopts a method of computing the center of training images and normalizing them. Does this mean that anyone who wants to evaluate a generative model needs to share all these training images? Also, considering the introduction of the auxiliary image captioning model called BLIP, the improvement in performance seems minimal. Do the authors believe it is worth the trade-off?

4) Question about Table 5: cI may have missed it, but was there an explanation for the last column of FID_clip in Table 5? What does it represent? Also, in Table 5, it seems that SaKLD is calculated for a single attribute. How is it computed?

5) Can PaKLD be proposed for more than two attributes? It would be great if judgments could be made for a wide range of attributes as shown in Figure 1 (b).

6) How should we interpret numerical values itself of the scores?  Although it may not be as interpretable as accuracy I'm still curious about the authors' insights based on their experience.

Limitations:
It seems that there are no particular specific limitations.

Rating:
6: Weak Accept: Technically solid, moderate-to-high impact paper, with no major concerns with respect to evaluation, resources, reproducibility, ethical considerations.
reviews.original_rating: 6: Weak Accept: Technically solid, moderate-to-high impact paper, with no major concerns with respect to evaluation, resources, reproducibility, ethical considerations.

reviews.summary: This is a very interesting research work. The main contribution of this paper is to consider the attribute information in the original training data when evaluating the quality of images generated by the model. There are two benefits to this approach: 1) determining whether the model can correctly imitate the distribution of the training data; 2) explaining which attributes the model does not perform well on. In implementing this idea, the authors found that directly calculating the CLIPScore between the image representation and the text representation of the attribute does not yield distinctive results. Therefore, they proposed Directional CLIPScore (DSC). The main idea of this approach is to move the reference point for calculating vector similarity to a more reasonable point. At the same time, they proposed two methods to apply DSC, one is Single attribute KL Divergence, and the other is Paired attribute KL Divergence (PaKLD) considering the combination of attributes.
reviews.strengths:
1. I think this research question is very interesting. It is very meaningful to use the attribute information of the dataset to help evaluate the quality of the model's generation.
2. The Directional CLIPScore (DSC) proposed by the authors is very concise and appears to be quite effective in the case study.
reviews.weaknesses:
1. In Section 3.3, I agree with the extraction of attributes using the BLIP and manual annotation methods. However, one method of extracting attributes is to generate an attribute list with GPT and then filter it with training data. I think the attribute list generated by GPT in advance may bring biases. The core of this paper is mainly to study the correlation between the model and the training data. However, if the list generated by GPT is not the most representative attribute, the results may be biased.
2. The experimental part in Sections 5.1-5.3 does not seem very convincing. The authors mainly verify that the proposed method can indeed be consistent with some expected experimental designs, but there is a lack of more convincing quantitative indicators to show that their proposed evaluation metrics are better than those proposed by others previous research works. I would prefer to see the authors analyze the correlation coefficient between their proposed evaluation metrics and human evaluation, as well as whether their evaluation metrics have improved in terms of correlation coefficient compared to previous evaluation indicators. This is my biggest concern for this work.

1. In Section 3.1, can the problem exhibited by CLIPScore be solved by directly calculating the Euclidean distance between E(x) and E(a) without relying on Directional CLIPScore?
2. Notation overload. The meanings of N used in Eq. (5) and Eq. (2) are different.

reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
This is a very interesting research work. The main contribution of this paper is to consider the attribute information in the original training data when evaluating the quality of images generated by the model. There are two benefits to this approach: 1) determining whether the model can correctly imitate the distribution of the training data; 2) explaining which attributes the model does not perform well on. In implementing this idea, the authors found that directly calculating the CLIPScore between the image representation and the text representation of the attribute does not yield distinctive results. Therefore, they proposed Directional CLIPScore (DSC). The main idea of this approach is to move the reference point for calculating vector similarity to a more reasonable point. At the same time, they proposed two methods to apply DSC, one is Single attribute KL Divergence, and the other is Paired attribute KL Divergence (PaKLD) considering the combination of attributes.

Strengths:
1. I think this research question is very interesting. It is very meaningful to use the attribute information of the dataset to help evaluate the quality of the model's generation.
2. The Directional CLIPScore (DSC) proposed by the authors is very concise and appears to be quite effective in the case study.

Weaknesses:
1. In Section 3.3, I agree with the extraction of attributes using the BLIP and manual annotation methods. However, one method of extracting attributes is to generate an attribute list with GPT and then filter it with training data. I think the attribute list generated by GPT in advance may bring biases. The core of this paper is mainly to study the correlation between the model and the training data. However, if the list generated by GPT is not the most representative attribute, the results may be biased.
2. The experimental part in Sections 5.1-5.3 does not seem very convincing. The authors mainly verify that the proposed method can indeed be consistent with some expected experimental designs, but there is a lack of more convincing quantitative indicators to show that their proposed evaluation metrics are better than those proposed by others previous research works. I would prefer to see the authors analyze the correlation coefficient between their proposed evaluation metrics and human evaluation, as well as whether their evaluation metrics have improved in terms of correlation coefficient compared to previous evaluation indicators. This is my biggest concern for this work.

Questions:
1. In Section 3.1, can the problem exhibited by CLIPScore be solved by directly calculating the Euclidean distance between E(x) and E(a) without relying on Directional CLIPScore?
2. Notation overload. The meanings of N used in Eq. (5) and Eq. (2) are different.

Limitations:
see weakness

Rating:
5: Borderline accept: Technically solid paper where reasons to accept outweigh reasons to reject, e.g., limited evaluation. Please use sparingly.
reviews.text:
## Hybrid Peer Review

**Summary:**
This paper presents a very interesting and novel research direction by proposing attribute-based interpretable evaluation metrics for generative models, addressing a significant gap in the field where existing metrics often lack interpretability. The core contribution is the introduction of Directional CLIPScore (DCS), an interpretable embedding designed to quantify image attributes relative to a dataset's center in CLIP space. The authors found that directly calculating CLIPScore between image and text representations of an attribute does not yield distinctive results, leading to the development of DCS to move the reference point for vector similarity. Leveraging DCS, the paper introduces two new metrics: Single attribute KL Divergence (SaKLD) for comparing individual attribute distributions, and Paired attribute KL Divergence (PaKLD) to assess the preservation of attribute relationships and combinations. The work demonstrates these metrics' ability to provide explanations for generative model performance and offers insights into the strengths and limitations of GANs and diffusion models, particularly highlighting DMs' weakness in color attribute generation.

**Strengths:**
1.  **Originality and Significance:** This research question is highly original and very meaningful, offering a fresh perspective by aligning evaluation with human-like attribute assessment. The core idea of transforming opaque embeddings into interpretable, attribute-specific scores (DCS) is novel and addresses a critical, often-missing component in generative model evaluation. The explicit focus on attribute relationships through PaKLD is also a strong contribution, offering a new dimension for evaluation. The proposed metrics provide deeper, actionable insights into model strengths and weaknesses, which can directly guide model development and improvement, thereby advancing the state of the art.
2.  **Conceptual Soundness:** The Directional CLIPScore (DSC) proposed by the authors is concise, well-motivated, and appears to be quite effective in the case study. It is empirically shown to outperform standard CLIPScore in accuracy for attribute quantification. The KLD-based metrics (SaKLD and PaKLD) logically follow from the DCS embeddings and are intuitive extensions. The conceptual illustration (Figure 1) is helpful, and the problem statement is clearly articulated.
3.  **Initial Validation Insights:** The toy experiments, such as correlated image injection and the necessity of PaKLD, effectively validate the proposed metrics' sensitivity and interpretability. The analysis of GANs versus Diffusion Models, particularly the insights into DMs' performance on color attributes, is well-supported and contributes valuable understanding to the generative model literature.

**Weaknesses:**
1.  **Limited Quantitative Validation and Comparison to Baselines:** My biggest concern for this work is the lack of more convincing quantitative indicators to show that their proposed evaluation metrics are better than previous research works. The experimental part in Sections 5.1-5.3 mainly verifies that the proposed method can indeed be consistent with some expected experimental designs, but there is a lack of rigorous validation. I would prefer to see the authors analyze the correlation coefficient between their proposed evaluation metrics and human evaluation, as well as whether their evaluation metrics have improved in terms of correlation coefficient compared to previous evaluation indicators. The validation experiments, while illustrative, are largely confined to "toy examples" on human face datasets and do not provide extensive, rigorous validation across diverse generative tasks or against a broader suite of established baselines.
2.  **Technical Clarity and Justification:** Several technical details require significant clarification. The "mean accuracy" presented in Table 1 for comparing CLIPScore and DCS is not precisely defined, making it impossible to assess the validity of the claimed superiority of DCS. Furthermore, the definition of CT in Equation (2) as the center of BLIP-generated captions for images, rather than directly from the defined attribute texts, lacks clear justification and may introduce unintended dependencies. Details on the Gaussian kernel density estimation process, including bandwidth selection and the method for sampling 10,000 points from the PDF, are minimal or absent, impacting reproducibility.
3.  **Experimental Setup Ambiguity and Inconsistencies:** The experimental setup in Section 5.1, the "Correlated Image Injection Experiment," is highly confusing. It states that two non-overlapping subsets of *FFHQ* are considered as 'training data X and generated images Y', implying both are real data, not generated. This contradicts the fundamental purpose of evaluating generative models against their outputs. Additionally, the experiment described for 'Necessity of PaKLD' (Section 5.2) contains a direct contradiction between the text (line 252: 'non-smiling men' and 'non-smiling women') and Figure 4's caption ('non-smiling men and smiling women'). Such inconsistencies severely undermine the credibility of the experimental results.
4.  **Attribute Selection Bias and Generalizability:** In Section 3.3, while the extraction of attributes using BLIP and manual annotation methods is reasonable, the method of generating an attribute list with GPT and then filtering it with training data may introduce biases. If the list generated by GPT is not the most representative attribute, the results may be biased, especially since the core of this paper is mainly to study the correlation between the model and the training data. Furthermore, the generalizability of the metrics beyond human face datasets (FFHQ, CelebA, MetFaces) is not thoroughly explored, and applying these metrics to diverse image domains where attributes are less clearly defined or extractable via text could prove challenging. The robustness of the metrics to variations in attribute selection methodology (e.g., specific phrasing of text prompts) also remains underexplored.
5.  **Computational Cost and Scalability:** The paper does not adequately address the computational overhead and scalability of the proposed approach. Calculating DCS for potentially thousands of images and many attributes, followed by kernel density estimation and KL divergence for all single and paired attributes, could be substantial, especially for very large datasets or high-dimensional attribute spaces, compared to simpler metrics like FID. This could be a practical concern for widespread adoption.
6.  **Dependency on Pre-trained Models and Missing Appendix:** The metrics are inherently tied to the representational biases of pre-trained models like CLIP and BLIP. Any biases in these foundational models will propagate directly into the proposed evaluation metrics, which should be explicitly discussed. Finally, the Appendix is referenced multiple times (lines 189, 276, 294) but is not included, which impedes a full understanding of key methodological choices and details.

**Questions:**
1.  **DCS Accuracy Definition:** Could the authors provide a more detailed explanation of how the "mean accuracy" in Table 1 for CLIPScore and Directional CLIPScore is calculated? Specifically, what constitutes a "positive sample with the highest score" for a given attribute, and what specific classification task is being performed to yield these accuracy values?
2.  **Theoretical Justification for DCS:** While empirical results show DCS outperforms CLIPScore, could the authors provide a more theoretical or intuitive explanation for why centering the vectors (Vx, Va) around CX and CT leads to more precise and interpretable attribute scores, beyond the observation of a "narrow distribution" for CLIPScore? Has sensitivity to the choice of CX and CT been investigated?
3.  **CT Justification:** Clarify the justification for using `CT = 1/N sum(ET(BLIP(xi)))` in Equation (2). Why is the mean of BLIP-generated captions for images the appropriate reference point for attribute texts, rather than a center derived directly from the attribute texts themselves?
4.  **Attribute Selection for Main Experiments:** For the comparative analysis and ablation studies, the paper mentions using N=20 attributes. Could the authors clarify which attribute selection method (BLIP, User, or GPT) was primarily used for these experiments, how the specific set of 20 attributes was chosen, and how "frequently appearing" is quantified for BLIP/GPT outputs?
5.  **Computational Cost:** Could the authors elaborate on the computational overhead (time and memory requirements) of calculating DCS, SaKLD, and PaKLD compared to existing metrics like FID, especially for large datasets and a high number of attributes?
6.  **KDE Details:** Provide full details on the Gaussian kernel density estimation process used for PDFs, including the bandwidth selection method and how the 10,000 points are sampled from each PDF for KLD calculation. How robust are the results to changes in these parameters?
7.  **Experimental Setup Clarification:** Resolve the ambiguity in Section 5.1: Is 'Y' a set of generated images from a specific model, or another real subset of FFHQ? If it's real, how does this experiment directly validate metrics for *generative model* evaluation? Also, please correct the contradiction between the text in line 252 and the caption of Figure 4 for the 'Necessity of PaKLD' experiment.
8.  **Notation Overload:** The meanings of N used in Eq. (5) and Eq. (2) are different. Please clarify or use distinct notations.
9.  **Generalizability:** How would the proposed metrics generalize to other domains (e.g., natural scenes, medical images, abstract art) where attributes might be less explicit, require different pre-trained models, or where text-image alignment is less straightforward?
10. **Missing Appendix:** The Appendix is referenced multiple times but is not included. Please provide the Appendix, as it is essential for understanding the complete methodology.

**Limitations:**
The authors adequately address several limitations, stating that their metrics might yield unreliable results when attributes in images are ambiguous (e.g., complex modern artworks) or when the generative model's output quality is significantly poor, making DCS measurement difficult. However, additional critical limitations include:
1.  The significant computational cost and scalability concerns for large datasets and numerous attributes, especially for PaKLD.
2.  The heavy reliance on pre-trained models like CLIP and BLIP, meaning the quality and biases of these foundational models will directly impact the interpretability and accuracy of the proposed metrics.
3.  The subjectivity and exhaustiveness of attribute selection, and the robustness to specific phrasing of attributes.
4.  The limited generalizability of the metrics, which are primarily validated on human face datasets, to more diverse image domains.
5.  The sensitivity of the metrics to hyperparameters, particularly the kernel bandwidth for KDE, which is not fully explored.
6.  The potential for the metrics, particularly PaKLD, to struggle with subtle or complex attribute relationships that are not easily expressible via simple text prompts.
7.  The lack of direct evaluation of a generative model against its training data in some of the validation experiments.

**Rating:**
5: Borderline accept: This is a technically solid paper with a very interesting and novel core idea, addressing a significant need for interpretable evaluation metrics in generative AI. The proposed Directional CLIPScore and the derived KL divergence metrics are conceptually sound and show promising initial results. However, the paper has notable weaknesses in its quantitative validation, clarity of experimental details, and justification of certain methodological choices. The lack of a thorough comparison against existing metrics, ambiguous experimental setups, and missing technical details (like KDE parameters and the Appendix) prevent a stronger acceptance. If these significant concerns regarding validation, clarity, and reproducibility can be addressed, the paper has the potential to be a strong contribution to the field.

reviews.summary:
This paper proposes a new metric to evaluate the quality and diversity of generated images based on
interpretable embeddings. To obtain the interpretable embeddings for selected attributes, the cluster
centers of the encodings of two separate encoders, one for images and the other for the text attributes
are calculated and the interpretable embeddings are the difference between the encodings of each
image and attribute and their respective cluster centers. The interpretable embeddings represent the
direction in which a particular embedding lies with respect to it’s cluster center. The CLIPScore
between the interpretable embeddings of an image and an attribute is calculated and is named as
’Directional CLIPScore’, since the interpretable embeddings represent the ’direction’.
The authors have proposed two metrics : 1. SaKLD - to quatify how closely the attribute distri-
bution in generated images matches with that of training images. 2. PaKLD - to quantify correlations
between different attributes. The KL divergence between the probability density functions of the
Directional CLIPScores for all the images in the train data and generated data.
Using SaKLD, the KL divergence between the attribute distribution in training and generated
images is calculated. PaKLD calculates the KL divergence similar to SaKLD, except that the presence
of a pair of attributes is required in the training and generated images.
reviews.strengths: The motivation for the idea is good and has a huge potential impact for improving evaluation of generative models.
reviews.weaknesses:
- Although, the motivation for developing this metric is valid, the overall methodology and the
experimental results are not convincing for the use of this metric. Also, the experiments performed
are inadequate and do not sufficiently justify how well the metric performs compared to the previously
proposed metrics. Additionally, previous metrics can directly evaluate quality of generation based
on the generated images alone, but this metric heavily relies on the attributes in the form of text
descriptions. Thus, it limits the applicability and generalizability of this metric.
- The proposed metrics use CLIPScore (which already exists) for interpretable embeddings and then
applies KL Divergence for the PDFs of the ClipScores of images and attributes, thus, showing limited
novelty.
- Most of the paper is easy to follow but some important parts like, how is the center of text attributes
calculated, results from table 1 (what does accuracy stand for) etc. are a bit ambiguous. There is a
lot of scope to improve the technical soundness of the paper. Although some of the popular metrics
1are mentioned, there has been a lot of work in the generative modelling domain which the literature
survey must cover. The proposed methodology is not very sound and is not well supported with the
experimental setup. The results are also not sufficiently explained. Diversity is the main motivation
for the paper as it is mentioned in the abstract but any theoretical or empirical work to support it is
completely missing.
- Motivation and methodology including the steps to evaluate generated samples is understandable but
some parts are ambiguous (please refer above comments).
- Based on the current state of the experiments, the contributions don’t seem to be significant as there
is not enough validation to support the claims.

 typos:
- Line 43 : Instead of ”Figure 1 (b)”, it should be Figure 1 (a)
- Line 168 : Section 3.3 First letters of all the words in the heading must be capital
Other remarks :
- Line 56 : Instead of ”If the model lacks essential attributes” the following sounds technically
correct ”if model lacks ”representation” of essential attributes”.
- Line 56-58 : This claim does not seem to be correct.
- Line 147 : Link to the specified figure is missing
- Figure 2 is never refered to in the text, is it an unnecessary figure?
- Line 253 : Generated set has non-smiling men and non-smiling women. But Figure 4 caption
says otherwise
reviews.questions:
1. If I understood correctly, the text and the image encoders are different? If yes, then
the embedding space of both models is going to be very different and will be influenced by the
downstream tasks that they are trained on. Would this metric always work for embeddings from
different models? Does it make sense to calculate similarity between embeddings from different
models?
2. Table 1 represents accuracy results for CS vs DCS. What is meant by evaluating ”positive
samples” in line 164 and 165? And what is meant by ”how well they are aligned”? Also, what
exactly is the accuracy calculated for?
3. In Section 3.1, there is a mention of ”each channel of the embedding being utilized”, how exactly
does this happen?
4. Line 196-197 claims that the generated distribution is desirable only if it has exactly same number
of samples as in the training set that has a particular attribute. Why should it be? Afaik, the
underlying distribution of latents represent semantic features of the data and the generative
model is responsible to generate novel samples that could be realistic but not necessarily show
exactly same features same number of times in the training set. So, is the assumption in the
mentioned lines appropriate? Can you prove this or maybe refer to some papers that provide
such guarantees?
reviews.limitations: limitations have been addressed
reviews.rating: 4: Borderline reject: Technically solid paper where reasons to reject, e.g., limited evaluation, outweigh reasons to accept, e.g., good evaluation. Please use sparingly.
reviews.confidence: 2: You are willing to defend your assessment, but it is quite likely that you did not understand the central parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work. Math/other details were not carefully checked.

Summary:

interpretable embeddings. To obtain the interpretable embeddings for selected attributes, the cluster
centers of the encodings of two separate encoders, one for images and the other for the text attributes
are calculated and the interpretable embeddings are the difference between the encodings of each
image and attribute and their respective cluster centers. The interpretable embeddings represent the
direction in which a particular embedding lies with respect to it’s cluster center. The CLIPScore
between the interpretable embeddings of an image and an attribute is calculated and is named as
’Directional CLIPScore’, since the interpretable embeddings represent the ’direction’.
The authors have proposed two metrics : 1. SaKLD - to quatify how closely the attribute distri-
bution in generated images matches with that of training images. 2. PaKLD - to quantify correlations
between different attributes. The KL divergence between the probability density functions of the
Directional CLIPScores for all the images in the train data and generated data.
Using SaKLD, the KL divergence between the attribute distribution in training and generated
images is calculated. PaKLD calculates the KL divergence similar to SaKLD, except that the presence
of a pair of attributes is required in the training and generated images.

Strengths:
The motivation for the idea is good and has a huge potential impact for improving evaluation of generative models.

Weaknesses:
- Although, the motivation for developing this metric is valid, the overall methodology and the
experimental results are not convincing for the use of this metric. Also, the experiments performed
are inadequate and do not sufficiently justify how well the metric performs compared to the previously
proposed metrics. Additionally, previous metrics can directly evaluate quality of generation based
on the generated images alone, but this metric heavily relies on the attributes in the form of text
descriptions. Thus, it limits the applicability and generalizability of this metric.
- The proposed metrics use CLIPScore (which already exists) for interpretable embeddings and then
applies KL Divergence for the PDFs of the ClipScores of images and attributes, thus, showing limited
novelty.
- Most of the paper is easy to follow but some important parts like, how is the center of text attributes
calculated, results from table 1 (what does accuracy stand for) etc. are a bit ambiguous. There is a
lot of scope to improve the technical soundness of the paper. Although some of the popular metrics
1are mentioned, there has been a lot of work in the generative modelling domain which the literature
survey must cover. The proposed methodology is not very sound and is not well supported with the
experimental setup. The results are also not sufficiently explained. Diversity is the main motivation
for the paper as it is mentioned in the abstract but any theoretical or empirical work to support it is
completely missing.
- Motivation and methodology including the steps to evaluate generated samples is understandable but
some parts are ambiguous (please refer above comments).
- Based on the current state of the experiments, the contributions don’t seem to be significant as there
is not enough validation to support the claims.

reviews.text:
**Overall Recommendation:** 4: Borderline reject: Technically solid paper where reasons to reject, e.g., limited evaluation, outweigh reasons to accept, e.g., good evaluation.

**Summary:**
This paper introduces a novel approach to address the lack of interpretability in generative model evaluation metrics by proposing attribute-based interpretable metrics. The core contribution is the "Directional CLIPScore" (DCS), an interpretable embedding derived from the difference between image and text attribute encodings and their respective cluster centers in CLIP space. This DCS is intended to quantify image attributes relative to a dataset's centroid, representing the 'direction' of an attribute. Leveraging DCS, the authors propose two new metrics: Single attribute KL Divergence (SaKLD), which quantifies how closely the attribute distribution in generated images matches that of training images, and Paired attribute KL Divergence (PaKLD), designed to quantify correlations between different attributes by assessing the preservation of attribute relationships. While the motivation for developing these metrics is strong and addresses a significant gap in the field, the overall methodology and experimental results, particularly the validation and generalizability, are not sufficiently convincing to fully support the claims.

**Strengths:**
The motivation for developing interpretable evaluation metrics for generative models is excellent and addresses a critical, often-missing component in the field. The paper offers a highly original perspective by proposing a departure from existing black-box approaches, with the concept of Directional CLIPScore (DCS) to create an attribute-driven embedding space being a novel contribution. The subsequent SaKLD and PaKLD metrics provide an intuitive framework for offering more fine-grained insights into generative model performance than traditional scalar metrics. This work has significant potential impact by providing deeper, actionable insights into model strengths and weaknesses, which could directly guide model development and improvement.

**Weaknesses:**

1.  **Limited and Ambiguous Experimental Validation:** Although the motivation is valid, the experimental results are largely insufficient and not convincing for the widespread use of these metrics. The paper's primary validation experiment in Section 5.1, the "Correlated Image Injection Experiment," describes using two non-overlapping subsets of *FFHQ* as 'training data X and generated images Y'. This setup, evaluating two *real* subsets rather than a generative model against its training data, fundamentally contradicts the stated purpose of evaluating generative models and severely undermines the credibility of the experimental validation for the metrics' primary intended use. The validation experiments are largely confined to "toy examples" on human face datasets, failing to provide extensive, rigorous validation across diverse generative tasks or against a broader suite of established baselines. Consequently, the contributions do not seem significant enough due to a lack of robust validation.

2.  **Lack of Technical Soundness and Clarity in Key Definitions:** Several critical technical details and definitions are ambiguous or lack clear justification, impacting the paper's soundness and reproducibility:
    *   The precise definition and calculation methodology for "mean accuracy" in Table 1, used to claim DCS's superiority over standard CLIPScore, are not provided, making it impossible to assess the validity of this crucial claim.
    *   The definition of CT in Equation (2) as the center of BLIP-generated captions for images, rather than directly from the defined attribute texts, lacks clear justification and may introduce unintended dependencies and biases.
    *   Details on the Gaussian kernel density estimation process used to derive the PDFs for DCS values, including the bandwidth selection method and how the 10,000 points are sampled from each PDF for KLD calculation, are absent. These are critical for the reliability and reproducibility of the KLD calculations.
    *   The paper references an Appendix multiple times (lines 189, 276, 294) but it is not included, which is a major oversight that impedes understanding of key methodological choices.
    *   Some parts of the paper, such as the explanation of "each channel of the embedding being utilized" in Section 3.1, remain ambiguous.

3.  **Core Conceptual Assumption Questionable:** The claim in lines 196-197, stating that a generated distribution is desirable only if it has exactly the same number of samples as in the training set that has a particular attribute, is problematic. Generative models are designed to learn underlying data distributions and produce novel, realistic samples, not necessarily to replicate the training set's feature frequencies exactly. This assumption about desirable generation needs stronger theoretical justification or empirical proof, or at least a more nuanced discussion, as it impacts the interpretation of the proposed KLD metrics.

4.  **Limited Novelty and Generalizability:** While the *idea* of interpretable evaluation is novel, the proposed metrics heavily rely on existing components like CLIPScore and KL Divergence. The specific novelty of DCS, beyond a re-centering technique, could be more explicitly contextualized against existing attribute quantification methods. Furthermore, the metrics' heavy reliance on attributes in the form of text descriptions, and thus on pre-trained models like CLIP and BLIP, limits their applicability and generalizability. The quality and inherent biases of these foundational models will directly impact the interpretability and accuracy of the proposed metrics, an aspect not fully explored. The generalizability beyond human face datasets (FFHQ, CelebA, MetFaces) is also not thoroughly explored, posing challenges for broader adoption in diverse image domains where attributes might be less explicit or text-image alignment less straightforward.

5.  **Inconsistencies and Minor Errors:** There are several inconsistencies and minor errors that detract from the paper's credibility:
    *   A direct contradiction exists between the text in line 252 ('non-smiling men' and 'non-smiling women') and the caption of Figure 4 ('non-smiling men and smiling women') for the 'Necessity of PaKLD' experiment. Such factual errors severely undermine confidence in the experimental results.
    *   Line 43: Instead of "Figure 1 (b)", it should be Figure 1 (a).
    *   Line 168: Section 3.3 heading requires capitalization for all main words.
    *   Line 56-58: The claim "If the model lacks essential attributes" followed by a specific assertion "This claim does not seem to be correct" needs technical refinement.
    *   Line 147: A link to the specified figure is missing.
    *   Figure 2 is never referred to in the text, suggesting it might be unnecessary.

**Detailed Comments and Questions:**

1.  **Clarity and Definitions:**
    *   Please provide a precise definition of "mean accuracy" as used in Table 1, along with the detailed methodology for its calculation. What constitutes a "positive sample" for a continuous DCS score, and what specific classification task is being performed?
    *   Clarify the justification for using `CT = 1/N sum(ET(BLIP(xi)))` in Equation (2). Why is the mean of BLIP-generated captions for images the appropriate reference point for attribute texts, rather than a center derived directly from the attribute texts themselves?
    *   In Section 3.1, what is meant by "each channel of the embedding being utilized"? Please elaborate on this mechanism.

2.  **Experimental Setup and Validation:**
    *   Resolve the ambiguity in Section 5.1: Is 'Y' a set of generated images from a specific model, or another real subset of FFHQ? If it's real, how does this experiment directly validate metrics for *generative model* evaluation?
    *   The paper mentions using N=20 attributes for comparative analysis. Could the authors clarify which attribute selection method (BLIP, User, or GPT) was primarily used for these experiments, and how the specific set of 20 attributes was chosen? Was it a fixed, generic set, or tailored per dataset?
    *   The current experiments mainly focus on facial characteristics. How would the proposed metrics generalize to other domains (e.g., natural scenes, medical images, abstract art) where attributes might be less explicit, require different pre-trained models, or where text-image alignment is less straightforward?

3.  **Technical Details and Reproducibility:**
    *   Provide full details on the Gaussian kernel density estimation process used for PDFs, including the bandwidth selection method and how the 10,000 points are sampled from each PDF for KLD calculation. How robust are the results to changes in these parameters?
    *   The Appendix is referenced multiple times (lines 189, 276, 294) but is not included. Please provide the Appendix, as it is essential for understanding the complete methodology and details (e.g., GPT questions, attribute analysis).

4.  **Conceptual and Theoretical Underpinnings:**
    *   Regarding the assumption in lines 196-197 about desirable generated distributions matching training attribute frequencies: Can you provide theoretical justification or references to papers that support this claim for generative models designed for novelty?
    *   Given that text and image encoders might be different and influenced by their downstream tasks, does it always make sense to calculate similarity between embeddings from different models in this manner?

5.  **Practical Considerations:**
    *   What are the computational costs (time and memory) associated with calculating DCS, SaKLD, and PaKLD compared to existing metrics like FID, especially for large datasets (e.g., 50,000 images) and multiple attributes? This information is crucial for practical adoption.

**Limitations:**
While the authors acknowledge limitations regarding ambiguous attributes and challenges with very poor generative models, several other critical limitations are not adequately addressed:
1.  **Dependency on CLIP/BLIP Biases:** The metrics are inherently tied to the representational biases of CLIP and BLIP models. Any biases in these foundational models will propagate directly into the proposed evaluation metrics.
2.  **Computational Cost:** The paper does not adequately address the computational overhead of calculating DCS for potentially thousands of images and many attributes, followed by kernel density estimation and KL divergence for all single and paired attributes. This could be substantial, especially for large-scale evaluations.
3.  **Sensitivity to Hyperparameters:** The choice of kernel bandwidth for KDE and the method of sampling points from the PDF are critical for KLD results. The absence of details on these choices and a discussion of their impact is a significant omission.
4.  **Attribute Selection Robustness:** While three methods are proposed, the robustness and generalizability of selected attributes across diverse datasets and domains are not fully explored. The reliance on external models (BLIP, GPT) introduces external dependencies whose impact needs further discussion.
5.  **Generalizability Beyond Specific Domains:** The metrics' applicability to diverse image domains beyond human faces, where attributes might be less explicit or text-image alignment is less straightforward, remains largely unexplored.

Summary:
This paper proposes a new metric to evaluate the quality and diversity of generated images based on
interpretable embeddings. To obtain the interpretable embeddings for selected attributes, the cluster
centers of the encodings of two separate encoders, one for images and the other for the text attributes
are calculated and the interpretable embeddings are the difference between the encodings of each
image and attribute and their respective cluster centers. The interpretable embeddings represent the
direction in which a particular embedding lies with respect to it’s cluster center. The CLIPScore
between the interpretable embeddings of an image and an attribute is calculated and is named as
’Directional CLIPScore’, since the interpretable embeddings represent the ’direction’.
The authors have proposed two metrics : 1. SaKLD - to quatify how closely the attribute distri-
bution in generated images matches with that of training images. 2. PaKLD - to quantify correlations
between different attributes. The KL divergence between the probability density functions of the
Directional CLIPScores for all the images in the train data and generated data.
Using SaKLD, the KL divergence between the attribute distribution in training and generated
images is calculated. PaKLD calculates the KL divergence similar to SaKLD, except that the presence
of a pair of attributes is required in the training and generated images.

Strengths:
The motivation for the idea is good and has a huge potential impact for improving evaluation of generative models.

Weaknesses:
- Although, the motivation for developing this metric is valid, the overall methodology and the
experimental results are not convincing for the use of this metric. Also, the experiments performed
are inadequate and do not sufficiently justify how well the metric performs compared to the previously
proposed metrics. Additionally, previous metrics can directly evaluate quality of generation based
on the generated images alone, but this metric heavily relies on the attributes in the form of text
descriptions. Thus, it limits the applicability and generalizability of this metric.
- The proposed metrics use CLIPScore (which already exists) for interpretable embeddings and then
applies KL Divergence for the PDFs of the ClipScores of images and attributes, thus, showing limited
novelty.

calculated, results from table 1 (what does accuracy stand for) etc. are a bit ambiguous. There is a
lot of scope to improve the technical soundness of the paper. Although some of the popular metrics

survey must cover. The proposed methodology is not very sound and is not well supported with the

for the paper as it is mentioned in the abstract but any theoretical or empirical work to support it is

- Motivation and methodology including the steps to evaluate generated samples is understandable but

- Based on the current state of the experiments, the contributions don’t seem to be significant as there

title: Residual Scheduling: A New Reinforcement Learning Approach to Solving Job Shop Scheduling Problem

reviews.summary: This paper proposes a deep reinforcement learning-based constructive heuristic to solve the (Flexible) Job Shop Scheduling Problem. An instance of the problem is represented as a graph and fed into a Graph Neural Network-based model which outputs a score for each candidate (operation-machine) pair. The model is trained with the REINFORCE algorithm with as baseline a classic Priority Dispatching Rule-based heuristic. The novelty of the paper lies in the update of the state after each action: the graph is updated by removing the operations which have already been executed to focus on the most relevant information, which is the residual operations and remaining times of the ongoing ones. The proposed approach is experimentally shown to outperform RL-based constructive heuristics on classic JSSP and FJSSP benchmarks.
reviews.strengths:
1. Sound and interesting idea of removing irrelevant information from the state 
1. The paper is fairly clear and I appreciated the illustrations Fig 1-3.
1. The model was proposed for the JSSP and easily adapted to the Flexible JSSP
1. The approach outperforms deep RL-based construction heuristics on classic benchmarks
reviews.weaknesses:
1. Limited novelty: the main contribution is the definition of the residual state at each step of the construction process by removing irrelevant operations and resetting the time reference. This seems to me an incremental improvement of the approach L2D [1], which already proposed a similar state graph representation and the use of the Graph Isomorphism Network architecture for the JSSP.
1. The proposed approach seems very specific to (F)JSSPs (state representation, baseline) and it's not clear what could be transferable to DRL heuristics for solving other optimization problems.
1. In the experiments, the presented non-learning-based baselines seem pretty weak: only greedy (see more in Questions)
1. Lots of English typos (I noted a few per page)

[1] C Zhang et al, Learning to Dispatch for Job Shop Scheduling via Deep Reinforcement Learning, Neurips 2020
reviews.questions:
1. Where dos the attributes of edges (L205) appear in the GNN model (Sec 3.3)?
1. The paper claims: “Interestingly, using RS, the average gaps are nearly zero for the collections with sizes larger than 100 … A strong implication is that our RS approach can be scaled up for job sizes and even reach the optimal for sufficient large job count.” Do you have an idea of why the model would work better on unseen large instances versus instances of the same size as the training ones? Does OR-tools return the optimal solutions for these larger instances? Can it be that the quality of the reference solutions decreases and therefore the “optimality” gap becomes smaller?
1. Are there stronger non-learning-based baselines for the JSPP other than the greedy PDR heuristics presented in Table 2? To be able to appreciate the performance of the proposed approach, it would be useful and more convincing to compare it to the best heuristics for this problem, beyond simple greedy ones. For example maybe [2]?
1. Appendix, Algorithm 1, Line 6:  to compute this makespan at state s_t, given action a_t, do you do a rollout of the current policy \pi_{\theta} with the updated parameter \theta? This would mean doing a rollout until the end of both the baseline and current policy at each step of the trajectory? How long did the training take?
1. How is the average computation time computed? (Table 13) In particular, was the policy applied to each instance individually or were instances batched? 
1. The discussion L322 about the time it takes for RS/L2D/ScheduleNet versus OR-Tools can be a bit misleading: OR-tools is an exact solver. If spends a lot of time proving the optimality of the solution. Probably it could return a good quality solution much faster if used as a heuristic. In addition it runs on CPUs and not GPUs therefore just comparing the time is not really fair. 

[2] CY Zhang, A tabu search algorithm with a new neighborhood structure for the job shop scheduling problem, Computers & Operations Research, 2007
reviews.limitations: Not explicitly addressed by the paper.
reviews.rating: 6: Weak Accept: Technically solid, moderate-to-high impact paper, with no major concerns with respect to evaluation, resources, reproducibility, ethical considerations.
